discussion_comments.comment_body: I believe we should treat LLONG_MIN and LLONG_MAX as implicit bounds, hence, clamp the results to [LLONG_MIN, LLONG_MAX ].
Overflow should be treated as a failure condition. Any INCREX operation resulting in overflow should be rejected without modifying the key.

@LiorKogan If we treat them as implicit bounds, the behavior will be inconsistent with `incrby` command.

```
127.0.0.1:6379> set mykey 9223372036854775800
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> incrby mykey 9223372036854775800
(error) ERR increment or decrement would overflow
```

Now that we have STRICT, 
I wonder if we should treat LLONG_MIN and LLONG_MAX as implicit bounds when STRICT is specified, and allow STRICT even if LBOUND/UBOUND are not specified.

This way, with STRICT, the behavior is consistent with INCRBY, but without STRICT, the result is clamped (with no error) and consistent with explicit bounds.
@LiorKogan @raffertyyu The concept of "STRICT" is not easy to understand. I keep constantly recalling what it is actually about. Maybe we can use "CLAMP" instead. Actually, the concept is the same.

or `[OVERFLOW FAIL|CLAMP]`
The default value is FAIL, same as INCR and INCRBYFLOAT.

I'm good with `XXX FAIL|CLAMP` though it is not just about overflow. 
`XXX FAIL` - reject a request due to an explicit OOR (`LBOUND`/`UBOUND` when specified) or an implicit one (overflow).
`XXX CLAMP` - clamp the result to `LBOUND`/`UBOUND` (when specified) or (LLONG_MIN/LLONG_MAX / -DBL_MAX/DBL_MAX (I think) when not specified).
`FAIL` could be the default.
Regarding naming: `OVERFLOW` is not a good name because it doesn't capture the explicit case. Maybe `OOR`?

But I guess it would be much more intuitive just to replace the `STRICT` flag with a `CLAMP` flag.
(and the default behavior - `STRICT` is equivalent to `FAIL` above).

WDYT?

Change to `ONBOUND FAIL|CLAMP`
Maybe there is another better choice for `ONBOUND`.

@sundb should we prevent passing same flags twice in the command? 
e.g. `increx x byint -500 byint 100`
done with https://github.com/redis/redis/pull/15045/commits/a7c7beba39fe2c9043b9ffda2331a0ca8f17815d

```
127.0.0.1:6379> set x -inf
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> increx x byfloat 0 UBOUND 100
1) "100"
2) "inf"
```
seems like we have some bugs when the key is `-+inf`. Please check if the behavior is correct.

Fixed by https://github.com/redis/redis/pull/15045/commits/d16890a3a2592c57ec67a978742c3a75d8024c97

```
127.0.0.1:6379> set x -inf
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> increx x byfloat 0 UBOUND 100
(error) ERR value is NaN or Infinity
127.0.0.1:6379> increx x byfloat 0
(error) ERR value is NaN or Infinity
127.0.0.1:6379> 
```

return error if value or increment is inf/-inf/nan

Maybe overflow here
```
increx a byint 9223372036854775800 ONBOUND CLAMP ubound -9223372036854775800
1) (integer) -9223372036854775800
2) (integer) 16
```

Should we return new_value + old_value instead? Old_value is zero if the key does not exist, which might be a bit confusing.

Or `new_value` + `nil` if actual_increment is not a valid 64-bit signed integer or a long double float. Users may need to calculate the actual increment themselves.

i was wondering if we should return old value instead of increment.
No matter how it is modified, the old value may still change from LLONG_MIN to LLONG_MAX, or vice versa, so there will always be an overflow.
ping @oranagra @LiorKogan

i think seeing how much was incremented is probably more useful to the user (easier to use).
and it only gets complicated on overflows and out of range inputs.
so it's tied to this [discussion](https://github.com/redis/redis/pull/15045#discussion_r3128689782) 

i don't think we should worry too much about the response in case of users that provide bad input.
if we'll decide to return an error, then problem solved!, if not, then we just need to make sure we understand and maybe document the outcome.
but i don't think i would make the command less user friendly in order to work around these edge cases.

i think that when the user intention cannot be served correctly (due to overflow or bad input) we should error.

so in my mind the only remaining question is what to do if the existing value is out of bounds.
i guess clamping is more useful (in case the user decides to change the bounds mid run).
and i guess we can treat that as a large increment, i.e. larger than the user asked for.
and i'd still return a delta rather than old value, and just document this case. i think it'll be more useful then return the old value.
Reply error if the increment is overflow in https://github.com/redis/redis/pull/15045/commits/e1b0b6e976a722c5d0fba67a3f8292fcc3bf7808
you shouldn't use redisSSLFree if you have a fatal error

can time be negative?
```suggestion
        if (time == RDB_LENERR || time > LLONG_MAX) {
```
I guess if the file is corrupted? Can never be saved negative, but this is a needed change for defensive programming. tnx.
not sure if we should check tsc_end is more than tsc_start, generally, it works well, chatgpt tells it should not happen on modern CPU
@ShooterIT Is there any risk with leap second?

Good catch — done in `ddcc0383f`. Added a `tsc_end <= tsc_start` guard that bails out to the POSIX clock path, matching the other calibration-failure branches in the same function. Agreed it shouldn't happen on modern invariant-TSC x86, but TSC skew across socket migration / virtualisation / firmware quirks can produce a non-monotonic sample pair; the `uint64_t` subtraction would otherwise wrap and we'd compute a nonsense tick rate rather than cleanly falling back.
@daemyung-lablup i think leap second does not work at this level, __rdtsc is CPU time stamp counter
@ShooterIT  Thanks.
